title: Generation via Classical Noise Reuploading
abstract: We propose a novel quantum generative model paradigm that fundamentally avoids the issue of extremely small post-selection probabilities present in previous models. Unlike existing methods that require multi-step noise addition and denoising, this paradigm enables direct single-step generation of quantum data, significantly improving generation efficiency while substantially reducing the complexity of training and quantum state preparation. Furthermore, by directly sampling classical noise to generate quantum states, the sampling process becomes easier to implement. Experimental results demonstrate that this paradigm outperforms existing quantum generative models in terms of generation quality.
summary: The paper introduces a new quantum generative model that uses classical noise re-uploading to generate quantum states in a single step. This approach avoids the exponential decay of success probability found in diffusion-based quantum models and demonstrates high efficiency in learning many-body properties like magnetization and entanglement.
main_problem: Existing quantum generative models like QuDDPM and QFM suffer from extremely low post-selection probabilities due to multi-step processes and high training/preparation overhead.
main_result: The proposed classical noise reuploading paradigm enables efficient single-step generation with a constant success probability of 1, outperforming existing models in generating quantum state distributions and learning many-body phases.
method: A quantum generative model using a data re-uploading circuit where classical noise is sampled and encoded via parameterized gates, optimized using the 2-Wasserstein distance via the Sinkhorn algorithm.
model_or_system: The model uses a parameterized quantum circuit (PQC) with noise re-uploaded at each step. It is tested on single-qubit Bloch sphere distributions and a 10-qubit Transverse-Field Ising Model (TFIM) with Hamiltonian H = -sum(Zn Zn+1) - g*sum(Xn).
key_observables: 2-Wasserstein distance (W2), magnetization (M), entanglement entropy (S), and expectation values like <Y>^2 and <Z>^2.
important_parameters: Number of circuit repetitions (P), transverse field strength (g), and classical noise distribution (1D Gaussian or uniform).
main_assumptions: The input noise dimension is assumed to be one to avoid multidimensional complexity.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the framework of noise sampling and re-uploading; Fig 2 shows training loss and metric evolution; Fig 3 compares Wasserstein distance and success probability against QuDDPM; Fig 4 compares magnetization and entanglement entropy distributions.
paper_structure: Introduction of the problem, description of the noise reuploading framework, implementation details (loss functions and optimization), experimental validation on single-qubit states, application to many-body systems (TFIM and entanglement growth), and comparative performance analysis.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Sampling: Classical noise is sampled from a 1D Gaussian or uniform distribution and inputted into the parameterized quantum circuit. (b) Generating: (single-qubit example) The resulting generated quantum states. The model is capable of generating distinct target distributions—specifically, a ring perpendicular to the Y-axis and another perpendicular to the Z-axis on the Bloch sphere—under the exact same circuit parameters, driven solely by different classical noise samples. Generation quality is evaluated using ⟨Y ⟩2 and ⟨Z⟩2, which approach the ideal value of 0 for successful generation. FIG. 3. (a) Wasserstein distance between the quantum states generated by our method and QuDDPM and those in the test set as the number of QuDDPM steps (or number of repeti- tions in our method) increases. (b) Success probability as the number of QuDDPM steps (or number of repetitions in our method) increases. For QuDDPM, the success probability de- creases exponentially with the number of steps, whereas for our method it remains 1.
FIG. 2. (a) Training loss curve. (b) Evolution of the metric ⟨Y ⟩2 as the number of training epochs increases.
FIG. 4. (a) The distribution of magnetization for the gen- erated states matches perfectly with that of the data states. (b) The entanglement entropy perfectly matches the target values.
